channel.title: Aravind Kumar Kamaraj
channel.description: Recent content on Aravind Kumar Kamaraj

item.title: Curriculum Vitae
item.description:
Aravind Kumar Kamaraj #

Computational Neuroscience · Mathematical Modelling
Email: aravindkumark@outlook.com
Website: https://www.aravind-kumar.net
ORCID: 0000-0001-7926-1120

Colour indicates disciplinary domain where relevant: Neuroscience , Control Systems , and Mechanics .

Research Focus #

  • Mechanistic models of seizure onset and termination in epilepsy
  • EEG biomarkers in epilepsy and Alzheimer’s disease
  • Sleep and circadian modulation of brain dynamics
  • Model–data fusion and interpretable computational frameworks

Academic Appointments #

Research Fellow in Mathematical Modelling and Model–Data Fusion
University of Surrey · May 2024 – present

item.title: Publications
item.description:
Colour indicates disciplinary domain where relevant: Neuroscience , Control Systems , and Mechanics .

Journal Articles #

  1. Kamaraj, A. K., Szuromi, M. P.
    Modelling dysfunction-specific interventions for seizure termination in epilepsy.
    npj Systems Biology and Applications (Nature Portfolio) (2026), 12(9).

  2. Quilter, E. E. V., Downes, S., Deighan, M. T., Stuart, L., Charles, R., Tittensor, P., Junges, L., Kissack, P., Qureshi, Y., Kamaraj, A. K., Brigden, A. L. C.
    A digital intervention for capturing the real-time health data needed for epilepsy seizure forecasting: formative codesign and the usability study protocol (the ATMOSPHERE study).
    JMIR Research Protocols (2024), 13(1), e60129.

item.title: Invited talks and events
item.description:
Upcoming talks and events #

  1. Modelling dysfunction-specific interventions for seizure termination in epilepsy.
    in the minisymposium ‘Mathematics in Treatment of Brain Disorders’ at British Applied Mathematics Colloquium, Norwich, UK.
    29 March - 01 April 2026.

  2. Sleep EEG signatures of epileptiform activity in ageing and Alzheimer’s Disease.
    at the Epilepsy Research Institute UK Navigator Symposium on ‘Late onset epilespy’, Liverpool, UK.
    30 April 2026.

item.title: About Me
item.description: I am Aravind, trained as an engineer and applied mathematician, and now working as a neuroscientist. At the core of my work is a long-standing curiosity about how systems evolve over time and why they behave the way they do. This naturally led me to dynamical systems theory, which forms the backbone of my research. Real-world systems are rarely linear, predictable, or noise-free — and that is precisely what makes them interesting. My work therefore focuses on nonlinear and stochastic dynamical systems, both as mathematical objects and as tools for understanding complex biological phenomena.
